# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Kubernetes Deployment that pulls our application image from Docker Hub. Use the latest tag so we always get the newest version automatically. This is for our development environment where we want continuous updates without manual intervention.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: apps/v1  # Kubernetes API Version, change as per your needs
kind: Deployment   # Kind of resource to be created (Deployments are the right choice here)
metadata:           # Metadata related information about this particular kind of object i.e., name and namespace in case different clusters or namespaces need it 
    labels:          # Labels for identifying objects that will contain these resources together with other similar purpose deployable entities, such as setting them up to run on the same host machine (optional)  
        app : nodejsapp       # Setting a label of "nodejsapp" in this case makes it easier and efficient to get all related resource instances  by using labels. It's also required for selectors later when selecting these resources with kubectl or other tools, i.e., the application pod
    name: nodejspool     # Setting a Name "nodejspool" here will be more descriptive and can give you some control over your resource names in terms of naming 
spec:               # Specifications about how to create this kind or object instances, e.g., number/type of pods (optional)  
    replicas :3        # Define the desired state for Pod(s). Here we set it as "replicaset" which will maintain at least 'n' but can go upto also more than n in your case to handle network partition issues and survive failures 
                      replicationController: true,            # (optional) Kubernetes API version used when enabling the Replication Controller. If not specified kubectl uses v1api by default which is very deprecated nowadays due its lack of features like health checks or readiness proxies as well since with newer versions
    selector :         # Objects to be selected, in case there are multiple pod replicas under the same app version and it needs a way selectors for communication within that set. 
        matchLabels:   # Label keys/values (optional) which you use when selecting your resources such as our application name "app" or similar -> nodejsapp, to know more on label names in Kubernetes documents https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/labels/
            app:nodejsapp  # The value of matchLabel should be your application' name like "applicationname" or similar for this property to function properly, you can define as per requirements and it makes the kubectl selector more flexible. It helps get pods in Kubernetes cluster that matches with our requirement
    template:          # Template describes how a Pod will look when created by deploying an application  -> container/image details or command to start containers, ports mapping etc->  like docker run image_name but if it'd be the latest tag then you don’t need any specific argument. The configuration of Kubernetes pods are set in template section which helps kubectl understand how a Pod is going for creation
        metadata:         # Metadata about this particular kind or object, e., name and namespace -> Name will be nodejspool (default), Namespace can also take place after deploying the application to different Kubernetes cluster by using 'kubectl get pods --namespace' command. Like kube-system are other namespaces that store system level information of Pod or Nodes
          labels:         # Label keys/values -> Setting up label name and values according your requirement ‘app’ : nodejsapp (same as before), 'name':nodejspool for more descriptive control over resources. Kubernetes automatically assigns the pod an IP address, which can be useful in a distributed system setup
        spec:             # Specifications about how to create this kind or object instances -> Container specifications such like Image name (Docker Hub image), Ports mapping and command for starting container –> docker run nodejsapp's DockerHub tag as our application. Also, define environment variables if any
          containers:     # Definition of a single Pod’ s specification with its own config details ->  Replicas can be set to match the pod instances required like in case we want 'n' number instance(pod) running here 1 (default), which is useful for managing load balancing and failover strategy.
          image: nodejsapp   # Here setting Docker Hub Image name as our application, latest tag or specific version also works but if not specified then will use the default/latest Kubernetes available images in your dockerhub account from registry where this app is present –> 'nodejspool'
          ports:           # Mapping of Pod’s port to host Port -> 1 (default), which allows connections between pod and external network like application. When a user requests access, it would be the mapped internal IP address on nodejsapp service in your case if you have exposed more than one container for communication
            containerPort: 3000    # If no specific port mapping is needed then leave this as default i.,e , 'nodejspool' (default) external and Internal traffic will be handled by the kube-proxy which automatically handles load balancing between pods. Container ports can either map to host Ports or use a Host IP for Ingress, in case we want ingress setup with annotations like "nginx/ingress". This would make our nodejsapp available at http://<PublicIP>:3000
